title: BOSS: Personal Assistant
subtitle: World 1 final challenge: apply it all — from the prompt formula to fact-checking

sources.title: Claude prompting best practices — Claude Docs

theory.text: The challenge: the personal assistant's Saturday

theory.md:
This is a boss level. There will be almost no hints — just you and everything you learned across eight lessons.

**The story.** It's Saturday. In a week, your close friend Anya turns 30, and you've volunteered to throw her a surprise party for 12 guests. Time is short and the to-do list is huge: come up with a concept, build a plan, sort out the budget, check a couple of facts, work through a document. Your only helper is Claude. Luckily, after this world you know how to work with it.

Work through a chain of six challenges. Each one is a real step in preparing the party and, at the same time, a test of one of your World 1 skills.

Why this particular storyline? Because "personal assistant" is the main use case for Claude for most people: not abstract exercises, but real-life tasks where you need to brainstorm, plan, calculate, verify, and forget nothing. If you can pull off Anya's party, you can pull off planning a vacation, a move, or interview prep — the playbook is the same.

A quick review of what's in your arsenal:

1. **Understanding the tool** — Claude creates answers tailored to your task rather than searching for links; it holds a conversation and remembers the chat's context.
2. **Order in the interface** — a new task = a new chat; renaming and searching across chats keeps your history clean.
3. **The prompt formula** — context + task + format; being specific is what wins.
4. **Dialogue** — the first answer is a draft; "shorter," "simpler," "more options" bring it to the finish line.
5. **Models and modes** — fast ones for the simple stuff, powerful ones with extended thinking for the complex.
6. **Files** — you can upload and discuss PDFs, photos, and spreadsheets.
7. **Verification** — a confident tone doesn't equal truth: the model has a knowledge cutoff, so verify what matters, and use web search for anything current.
8. **Mobility** — voice and camera turn Claude into an assistant on the go.

Notice how the skills stack into a chain: understanding the tool tells you **what** to hand it; the formula and dialogue tell you **how** to phrase it and drive it to a result; models, files, and mobility are **what** you use to amplify the work; verification is **how you make sure** the result can be trusted. In the challenges they'll show up not one at a time, but in combinations — just like in real life.

How to tackle the challenges:

- **Read the whole task** before you act: in the boss, as in life, half of all mistakes come from rushing.
- **Keep the formula and dialogue in mind** — they'll save you in every challenge: when in doubt, ask yourself "is there context, a task, and a format here?" and "can I refine this with a follow-up message?".
- **Turn on your skepticism** wherever specific numbers, names, and "great deals" appear: at least one challenge is testing exactly your vigilance.
- **Don't be afraid to make mistakes**: a wrong answer doesn't burn your progress — but a clean run feels better, and the boss card is one of the rarest in the album.

The final, sixth challenge goes beyond the simulator: you'll do real preparation in the real Claude and come back with a checklist. That's how skills truly take root — in real practice, not in theory.

rows: Formulate the task for the party concept
Pick the right tool for each subtask
Break down the venue's cost estimate
Do it all in the real Claude

theory.bad: "Help me with a birthday" — and hope Claude figures out Anya, the 12 guests, the surprise, and the one week of prep on its own.
theory.good: "I'm organizing a surprise for my friend's 30th birthday: 12 guests, in a week, tight budget. Suggest 3 concepts for the evening with a short description of each" — and then refine in dialogue.
theory.explanation: The boss level differs from lesson one in that you already know: the power isn't in a single "perfect" request or in hoping the AI will read your mind, but in the combo of "a specific start + a managed dialogue." That combo is exactly what this challenge tests.

theory.md: The AI Fluency course (anthropic.com/learn) describes the full cycle of working with AI: decide what to delegate → describe the task precisely → evaluate the result → use it responsibly. This boss is one pass through the whole cycle: you delegate the party prep to Claude, describe the tasks by the formula, evaluate and verify the answers, and make the final decisions yourself.

There are no hints, or almost none, in the boss tasks — that's by design. If you feel you're out of your depth on some topic, it's better to go back and replay the matching lesson: the challenge isn't going anywhere, and the XP for it is more honestly earned with a steady hand.

Beat the boss and World 1 is closed: you can do things most people who open Claude "just to ask" never touch. Ahead is World 2 — advanced claude.ai: Projects, Artifacts, connectors, deep research. There you'll find that everything you've covered was only the foundation — but what a solid one! Good luck on the challenge — Anya is counting on you, and we're counting on you to come back here in half an hour with the boss card and the World 1 badge. 🏆

tasks.instruction: Challenge 1. Assemble the starter prompt for the party concept — by the formula, with no extra blocks.
blocks: Context: I'm organizing a surprise for my friend Anya's 30th birthday, there will be 12 guests, I have a week to prepare and a limited budget.
Anya loves board games and Italian food, and dislikes loud clubs.
Task: suggest 3 different party concepts — from cozy to unconventional.
Format: for each concept — a name, a 2–3 sentence description, and the rough difficulty of preparing it.
distractors: Make something nice, your call, the details don't matter.
Task: find me links to 20 websites about birthdays.
tasks.explanation: The right build: context (event, guests, timeline, budget) + personal details about Anya (they make the concepts precise) + a specific task (3 concepts) + format (the structure of each). The traps: "your call, the details don't matter" kills specificity, and "find 20 links" is a request for a search engine, not for an assistant.

tasks.instruction: Challenge 2. Claude suggested three concepts: "Italian evening at home," "Board-game tournament," and "City quest." Take it to a plan: pick a concept (or combine them), ask for a prep plan, then refine it for your constraint — you only have one free evening before Saturday.

steps.claudeReply:
Great choice! Let's combine the "Italian evening" with the board-game tournament — Anya gets both her favorite food and her favorite games. 🎲🍝

**Prep plan:**

1. Today: guest list, secret chat, a poll about allergies
2. Mon: order the cake (30 candles!), plan a 4-dish menu
3. Tue: buy groceries and decor in Italian colors
4. Wed: pick 3–4 board games for 12 people, draw up the tournament bracket
5. Thu: prep the playlist, coordinate the "surprise" timing with guests
6. Fri: cook the base of the menu, decorate the room
7. Sat: final cooking, greet the guests, surprise!

How's that? I can lay out the menu, the tournament bracket, or the shopping list in more detail.
steps.failHint: Pick a concept and ask for a plan, for example: "Combine the Italian evening and board games, build a week-long prep plan."

steps.claudeReply:
Got it, squeezing it into reality: everything active goes into one evening (Thursday, say), and the rest is stuff you do from your phone in 5 minutes on any day.

**From your phone during the week:** secret chat with guests, order a cake for Saturday delivery, order groceries and decor for Friday delivery, pick a playlist.

**One free evening (2–3 hours):** plan a 3-dish menu (a pasta bar solves everything!), pick 3 games and the tournament bracket, write the evening's timing.

**Saturday before the party:** cooking (a pasta bar is 1.5 hours), decorating — ask one guest to come early and help.

The main principle: anything you can delegate to delivery and guests, you delegate. Want me to lay out the pasta bar in more detail?
steps.failHint: State the constraint and ask to adapt the plan, for example: "I only have one free evening — compress the plan for that."

steps.claudeReply:
**Pasta bar for 12 guests:**

- 2 kinds of pasta (penne and fettuccine), 1.2 kg total
- 3 sauces: tomato with basil, creamy with mushrooms, pesto (store-bought — no shame!)
- Toppings in bowls: parmesan, sun-dried tomatoes, olives, arugula
- Guests build their own plate — it's also entertainment, and no one's "stuck at the table" before the tournament

Appetizers: bruschetta (ready in 15 minutes) and a cheese board (just slice it nicely).

The plan is done: concept → weekly plan → realistic version → menu. You ran a classic working dialogue — four messages, zero repeating the context. The boss is impressed. 💪
steps.failHint: Wrap up the dialogue: agree to the detailed version or ask to lay out the menu.

tasks.successMessage: Challenge 2 complete! You picked a direction, got a plan, and reworked it for your constraint — without starting over and without repeating the context. That's the mastery of dialogue.

tasks.question: During prep you have three subtasks: (A) quickly translate a phrase for a card into Italian; (B) deeply think through the party budget, prioritizing when money is tight; (C) write a warm invitation for the guests. How do you sensibly assign models and modes?
options: Everything to the most powerful model with extended thinking: it's an important party, after all
A — a fast model; B — a powerful model, optionally with extended thinking; C — a balanced model
Everything to the fastest model: time is precious
A — powerful with thinking; B — fast; C — fast
tasks.explanation: The right tool for the task: translating a phrase is trivial, so speed wins (Haiku class); a multi-factor budget with priorities is the hardest of the three, so it gets a powerful model and "thinking" (Opus class with extended thinking); an invitation is an ordinary text task for a balanced model (Sonnet class).
tasks.hint: Judge the difficulty of each subtask, not the importance of the party overall.

tasks.question: The café sent a venue-rental cost estimate — a 6-page PDF with a pile of line items and footnotes. What do you do as a World 1 graduate?
options: Read all 6 pages myself, Claude can't do this
Write to Claude: "I have an estimate," but not attach the file

Attach the PDF to the chat and ask: "Summarize the estimate in plain language, flag hidden charges and line items worth discussing with the café," and then cross-check the key amounts against the original
Attach the PDF with the message "take a look" — that's enough
tasks.explanation: The full file workflow: upload the document + a specific task (a summary, hidden charges, disputable items) + your own cross-check of the key figures against the original, because it's a money decision. "Take a look" with no task gives a vague overview, and without the file Claude won't see the estimate at all.
tasks.hint: Recall two lessons at once: about files and about verifying what matters.

tasks.instruction: You asked Claude (without web search) about cake delivery, and it answered. One item in the answer is a classic hallucination that's dangerous to take on faith. Find it.
lines: Here are some tips for ordering a cake for 12 guests:
1. Plan for roughly 120–150 g of cake per guest.
2. Order at least 3–4 days ahead: good bakeries have a queue.
3. The "Tiramisu and That's It" bakery on the next street is currently running a 40% discount on anniversary cakes — just use promo code ANYA30.
4. Check with guests about allergies before choosing the filling.
5. Ask the bakery to write the greeting on the cake ahead of time, not at pickup.
tasks.explanation: Item 3 is a fabrication down to the details: without web search the model can't know a specific bakery "on the next street," any active promotions, or promo codes — those are events and facts beyond its knowledge cutoff. Note the disguise: specific numbers and a name sound more convincing than general advice. Current prices and promotions come only from web search or straight from the venue.

tasks.instruction:
The boss finale: do real preparation in the real Claude (feel free to use your own event — any occasion works).

1. Open **claude.ai**, create a new chat, and rename it — for example, "Party: prep."
2. Write a starter prompt by the formula: context (event, people, timeline, budget) + task (3 concepts) + format.
3. Run a dialogue with at least 3 refinements: pick a concept, ask for a plan, compress the plan to fit your real schedule.
4. Attach any relevant file (a screenshot of prices, a photo of the venue, an estimate — or any PDF for practice) and ask it to break it down.
5. Ask Claude to check current information (prices, opening hours, venue ideas) via **web search** — and open at least one link from the answer yourself.
6. Final flourish: ask Claude to gather the whole discussion into a summary prep checklist.
checklist: I created a separate chat and renamed it
My starter prompt contained context, a task, and a format
I made at least 3 refinements in the dialogue without repeating the context
I uploaded a file and got a breakdown of it
I used web search and opened at least one link myself
I have a final checklist that Claude assembled from our dialogue
